prompt: Match each default port number to the protocol that uses it.
explanation: HTTPS uses 443, SSH uses 22, DNS uses 53, and NTP synchronizes time on UDP 123. Knowing default ports speeds up both configuration and troubleshooting.

prompt: Place the troubleshooting methodology steps in the correct order.

steps.text: Establish a theory of probable cause
Test the theory to determine cause
Establish a plan and implement the solution

explanation: The methodology runs from identifying the problem through testing a theory before fixing, then verifying and finally documenting — the two steps most often skipped.

prompt: Match each connector to the media it terminates.
explanation: LC terminates fiber, RJ45 terminates twisted-pair Ethernet, RJ11 is for phone lines, and BNC is a coaxial connector.

prompt: Match each protocol to its default port.
explanation: FTP uses 21, Telnet 23, SMTP 25, and RDP 3389. Knowing default ports speeds configuration and troubleshooting.

prompt: Match each attack to the control that best mitigates it.
explanation: Dynamic ARP Inspection stops ARP spoofing, DHCP snooping blocks rogue DHCP, port security limits MAC flooding, and strong wireless auth (WPA3/802.1X) defeats evil twins.

prompt: Place the DHCP lease (DORA) steps in order.
explanation: The client Discovers, the server Offers, the client Requests the offered address, and the server Acknowledges (DORA).

prompt: Match each command-line tool to what it is best used for.

targets.label: Show each hop along the path

explanation: ping tests reachability, traceroute shows the path, nslookup/dig query DNS, and ipconfig/ifconfig show local IP settings.

prompt: Place these change-management steps in a sensible order.
explanation: Changes are requested, assessed and approved, tested, implemented in a window, then verified and documented — with a rollback plan ready throughout.

prompt: A host can ping an IP address but cannot resolve a hostname. Type one DNS lookup tool, then type the common ICMP reachability test command.
objective: Given a scenario, troubleshoot common cable, connectivity, and software issues related to networking
explanation: nslookup or dig tests DNS resolution. ping is a basic ICMP reachability test.

prompt: A conference room switch port is down after a furniture move. Categorize each observation by the most likely fault area.
objective: Given a scenario, troubleshoot common cabling and physical interface issues

categories.label: Cabling or termination
categories.description: Copper/fiber media, connector, pinout, or patch lead fault.

categories.label: Interface configuration
categories.description: Switch port setting, VLAN, speed/duplex, or administratively disabled port.

categories.label: Physical environment
categories.description: Power, damage, labeling, rack, or workspace condition.

items.text: Link LED stays dark with two known-good laptops
items.detail: The same patch cable fails at this wall jack but works elsewhere.

items.text: Switch log says the port is administratively down
items.detail: No errors increment while the interface is disabled.

items.text: The cable run is pinched under a table leg
items.detail: The drop was moved during room rearrangement.

items.text: The port comes up but clients receive the wrong subnet
items.detail: The switchport is assigned to the guest VLAN.

items.text: Patch panel labels no longer match the room map
items.detail: Technicians cannot identify the correct drop without toning.

explanation: Dark links with known-good devices point to cabling or termination, disabled ports and wrong VLANs are interface configuration issues, and crushed cables or bad labels are physical-environment problems that must be corrected before deeper protocol troubleshooting.
